perturbation.original_text: 12.4 Applicable Law. This Agreement shall be construed and enforced in accordance with, and the rights of the parties shall be governed by, the laws of the State of New York and the laws of the United States applicable therein. Any and all disputes arising under this Agreement, whether as to interpretation, performance or otherwise, shall be subject to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Colorado and each of the parties hereto hereby irrevocably attorns to the jurisdiction of the courts of such province.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.4 Applicable Law. This Agreement shall be construed and enforced in accordance with, and the rights of the parties shall be governed by, the laws of England and Wales. Any and all disputes arising under this Agreement, whether as to interpretation, performance or otherwise, shall be subject to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales and each of the parties hereto hereby irrevocably attorns to the jurisdiction of the courts of such province.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the clause specifying that the laws of the State of New York and the laws of the United States are applicable, replacing them with the laws of England and Wales. This introduces a legal risk because the agreement involves a company organized under the laws of Colorado, and disputes might arise where US law provides consumer protections or regulations that are absent in English law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Dodd-Frank Act contains several provisions aimed at protecting consumers from unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices in the financial sector. By removing the application of US law and specifying English law instead, the agreement potentially avoids these consumer protection mandates, which is a legal contradiction if the agreement's activities impact US consumers or markets. 12 U.S. Code § 5531 specifically addresses the prohibition of unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the application of New York and United States law with the laws of England and Wales. This directly contradicts the potential application of the Dodd-Frank Act, specifically concerning consumer protection mandates outlined in 12 U.S. Code § 5531, as described in the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet includes the subheading 'Part C—Specific Bureau Authorities 5531. Prohibiting unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices,' indicating the existence of US laws designed to protect consumers, which are potentially bypassed by the change in governing law. The change introduces a legal contradiction as it eliminates US consumer protection mandated by law.

perturbation.original_text: So long as HOC is entitled to nominate and maintain a director pursuant to this Article 5, the Company shall indemnify each current and former HOC Director and shall maintain director's and officer's liability insurance for the benefit of each such director, with the same rights and benefits as are accorded the directors of the Company generally.
perturbation.changed_text: So long as HOC is entitled to nominate and maintain a director pursuant to this Article 5, the Company shall indemnify each current and former HOC Director.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement for the Company to maintain director's and officer's liability insurance for the HOC Director. This is a legal contradiction because under Delaware law, where many corporations are incorporated, indemnification and insurance are crucial for protecting directors from liabilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 Del. C. § 145 allows (and in some cases mandates) corporations to indemnify directors and officers and to purchase insurance on their behalf. Omitting the insurance provision weakens the protection for the HOC director, potentially contradicting expectations and norms established under Delaware law, especially if the company is later found to have operations affecting Delaware.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a director may provide that it is i
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates director's and officer's liability insurance, while the changed text omits this requirement. The Law Explanation states that 8 Del. C. § 145 allows (and in some cases mandates) corporations to purchase insurance on their behalf. Although the provided scraped snippet only contains § 141, the Law Explanation makes it clear that Delaware law acknowledges the importance of director's insurance. Therefore, removing the insurance provision weakens the protection for the HOC director and creates a potential legal contradiction, falling under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition of removing essential information and creating legal loopholes.

perturbation.original_text: (d) The Company shall, to the extent and for so long as HOC Entities hold at least 14.5% of the Shares on a non-diluted basis, upon HOC's request, permit representatives of the HOC Entities to have access to the site and any of the premises where the business and operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries are conducted and access and duplicating rights (and use commercially reasonable efforts to cause persons or firms possessing such documentation or information to give similar access and duplicating rights) to the Company and its Subsidiaries' books of account and records and such other documents, communications, items and matters, within the knowledge, possession or control of the Company, which HOC may reasonably request, at HOC's own cost (other than those it is permitted to examine and make copies of free of charge pursuant to applicable Laws) provided that, except to the extent the information can be provided in the necessary course of business of the Company, acting reasonably including to provide HOC Entities with information to assist the HOC Entities and their advisors with the preparation of the financial statements for such HOC Entities, nothing herein shall require the Company to provide HOC Entities with any information which would constitute a material fact with respect to the Company which has not been generally disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) The Company may, at its discretion, permit representatives of the HOC Entities to have access to the site and any of the premises where the business and operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries are conducted and access and duplicating rights to the Company and its Subsidiaries' books of account and records and such other documents, communications, items and matters, within the knowledge, possession or control of the Company, which HOC may reasonably request. Nothing herein shall require the Company to provide HOC Entities with any information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the mandatory aspect of providing access to information, making it discretionary. It also removes the exceptions that allowed access even to material non-public information if needed for financial reporting. This change introduces a legal contradiction because securities laws require transparency and access to certain information for shareholders with significant holdings.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 prohibits the making of materially false or misleading statements or omissions in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. While this section is about fraud, the removal of the obligation to provide access to information could lead to situations where HOC is disadvantaged due to lack of access to information, potentially creating a scenario that could violate the spirit, if not the letter, of securities laws designed to prevent insider trading and ensure fair markets. Especially when HOC is represented on the board, restricting access to information needed to fulfill their duties could constitute a legal problem.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modification changes a mandatory obligation for the Company to provide HOC Entities with access to information to a discretionary one. This contradicts securities laws, specifically 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which aims to prevent manipulative or deceptive practices in the purchase or sale of securities. While the snippet focuses on fraudulent statements or omissions, the changed text's removal of mandatory access to information could disadvantage HOC, potentially creating a situation that undermines the spirit of fair markets and preventing manipulative schemes. The original contract ensures the HOC entities had rights to access the data needed to ensure securities were handled fairly, which has been removed.
